did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model combined with Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) texture features to classify dermoscopic images for the detection of melanoma and Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model with Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) features for melanoma and BCC detection. It includes specific sections on methodology (data preparation, feature extraction, model development), experimental results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity metrics on training and testing datasets), and a conclusion summarizing new findings. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICICyTA), it reports original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: GLCM-based Texture Features and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) for Accurate Detection of Melanoma and Basal Cell Carcinoma
journal: 2024 International Conference on Intelligent Cybernetics Technology & Applications (ICICyTA)
brief_summary: This study developed an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model utilizing Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) texture features to classify dermoscopic images for the simultaneous detection of melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), and normal skin. The dataset comprised images sourced from Hasan Sadikin Hospital Bandung, external research data, and Telkom University, with images resized to 224x224 pixels. The model's performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score metrics across training and testing phases with varying GLCM neighborhood distances. The best-performing model achieved an average accuracy of 83.33%, sensitivity of 83.07%, specificity of 91.54%, and F1-score of 83.10% on the training dataset, and 82.22% across all metrics on the testing dataset. The results demonstrate that GLCM features effectively enhance ANN classification performance, offering a computationally efficient alternative for skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the primary classes for detection and classification using the ANN model. Included in the dataset from Hasan Sadikin Hospital Bandung (16 images) and external research data. Also used in application testing (10 photos).

One of the primary classes for detection and classification using the ANN model. Included in the dataset from Hasan Sadikin Hospital Bandung (45 images) and external research data. Also used in application testing (10 photos).
The benign/control class used for classification. Images were collected from students at Telkom University.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic category of dermoscopic images (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, or Normal Skin)
all_task_types.details: The paper develops an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model to classify dermoscopic images into three discrete diagnostic categories: melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), and normal skin. The task is explicitly described as 'classification' and 'detection' of these specific disease labels. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score on a training and testing dataset.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopic Dataset (Hasan Sadikin Hospital, External Research, Telkom University)
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset of 225 dermoscopic images resized to 224x224 pixels, sourced from three locations: Hasan Sadikin Hospital Bandung (melanoma and BCC), external research data [35] (melanoma and BCC), and Telkom University students (normal skin). The dataset was augmented to 600 images (200 per class) for training.

use.training: Used for training the ANN model with GLCM features. The original 225 images were augmented to 600 images (200 per class) for the training phase.
use.validation: Used for validation via 5-fold cross-validation on the training data.
use.testing: Used for final testing of the best model (20% of the original dataset, i.e., 45 images).
use.other: An additional set of 20 clinical photos (10 melanoma, 10 BCC) from the hospital was used for application testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the ANN model on the training dataset by splitting the data into five subsets, training on four, and validating on the remaining one, then averaging the results.
To address the limited amount of data by artificially increasing the dataset size to 200 images per class for the training phase.
To calculate sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score by analyzing true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score) and cross-validation, but no statistical significance testing was conducted.

Training Dataset (GLCM d=2, Best Model).notes: The best model was obtained at d=2 in fold 3. The model with d=2 showed the highest average accuracy and sensitivity among all neighborhood distances (d=1 to 5) and the most consistent performance across folds.

Testing Dataset (GLCM d=5, Best Model).notes: The model with d=5 achieved the best overall performance on the test dataset among all neighborhood distances. The model with d=4 showed the lowest performance (Acc: 77.78%, Sen: 77.78%, Spec: 88.89%, F1: 77.24%).

Testing Dataset (GLCM d=1).notes: Best fold for d=1 was fold 4.

Testing Dataset (GLCM d=2).notes: Best fold for d=2 was fold 3.

Testing Dataset (GLCM d=3).notes: Best fold for d=3 was fold 2.

Testing Dataset (GLCM d=4).notes: Best fold for d=4 was fold 4. This showed the lowest performance among all neighborhood distances.

Comparison with State-of-the-Art.notes: The proposed model's accuracy (82.22%) is higher than Maurya et al. and Kavitha & Suruliandi, but lower than Javaid et al. and Afandi et al. The proposed model offers a computationally efficient alternative to the CNN-LSTM ensemble.

architecture_details.structure: Weighted directed graph consisting of an input layer, hidden layers, and an output layer
architecture_details.neuron_operation: Each neuron calculates the weighted sum of its inputs and applies a non-linear activation function (e.g., sigmoid or ReLU)
architecture_details.connections: Weighted connections between neurons in previous and current layers

training_details.data_augmentation: Augmented to 200 images per class (total 600) due to limited original data (225 images)
training_details.input_features: GLCM texture features (Contrast, Correlation, Energy, Homogeneity) extracted using 4 angles (0°, 45°, 90°, 135°) and 5 neighborhood distances (d=1 to 5)

classification_or_output_head.output_activation: Linear combination of last layer outputs, potentially passing through an activation function depending on the task

preprocessing_techniques: Grayscale conversion (grayscale process of the image data)
Data augmentation (increasing dataset size to 200 images per class for training)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Due to the limited amount of original data (225 images), training data augmentation was conducted to balance the classes. The dataset was augmented to 200 images per class (Melanoma, BCC, and Normal Skin) for the training phase, resulting in a total of 600 training images.

conclusions: The proposed Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model utilizing Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) texture features demonstrates strong potential for accurately classifying dermoscopic images into melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), and normal skin categories.
The integration of GLCM features effectively enhances the classification performance of the ANN model, addressing limitations in feature extraction techniques found in previous studies.
The model achieved an average training accuracy of 83.33%, sensitivity of 83.07%, specificity of 91.54%, and F1-score of 83.10% using GLCM with a neighborhood distance (d) of 2.
On the testing dataset, the model with GLCM neighborhood distance d=5 achieved the best overall performance with 82.22% accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score.

The study highlights the effectiveness of using a multicultural dataset (sourced from Hasan Sadikin Hospital Bandung, external research, and Telkom University) to enhance model generalizability and address the challenge of limited data availability.
The proposed method offers a computationally efficient and practical diagnostic alternative for skin cancer detection compared to more complex deep learning architectures.

The simultaneous detection of both melanoma and BCC using GLCM texture analysis provides a flexible and clinically relevant diagnostic process, unlike previous studies that focused on single cancer types.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small original dataset (225 images total) which was artificially augmented to 600 images for training, raising concerns about data leakage or overfitting if augmentation techniques were not strictly separated from the test set.
External validation is limited; the testing set consists of only 45 images (20% of the original dataset), which is insufficient to robustly evaluate generalizability.

The model demonstrates inconsistent performance across folds, particularly for Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), with sensitivity dropping as low as 48.78% in fold 4, indicating instability in detecting this specific class.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (p-values) to validate the differences in performance between models or folds.
Clinical validation is absent; the model was tested on a held-out subset of the training data and a small set of clinical photos, but not on an independent, external clinical cohort.

The dataset sources are heterogeneous (hospital images, external research data, and normal skin from university students), which may introduce population bias and affect the model's ability to generalize to diverse clinical populations.

The proposed model's accuracy (82.22%) is lower than several cited state-of-the-art methods (e.g., Afandi et al. at 97.97%), suggesting that the GLCM-ANN approach may be less effective than more complex deep learning architectures for this task.
